findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.suggestion: You need a dedicated slide that shows how your product actually works and why it's better than what exists today. For example, if you're in logistics, show a side-by-side comparison: "Traditional approach = 5 manual handoffs and 48-hour delays" versus "Our platform = automated matching and 4-hour delivery," then briefly explain the 2-3 key features that make that possible (like your proprietary routing algorithm or real-time tracking).

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
Does the deck include a Financials slide?
Does the deck include a Traction slide?
Does the deck include a Market slide?
Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
findings.suggestion: You need a dedicated slide showing who your direct competitors are and what makes you different. Create a simple 2x2 or comparison table with 3-4 real competitors (name them specifically—don't be vague) and map out how you differ on the dimensions that matter most to your customers, like price, speed of implementation, or specific features you uniquely offer.

findings.label: Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?

Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.
findings.evidence: [S6] Zeevaa Financial Model — Revenue_Model_Projection_Final.pdf (Internal) Used for Slides D, G, H, J — SOM $22.3M by 2030, $2.8M Year 1 target, unit economics, subscription assumptions

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of customer discovery or validated demand — such as interviews, surveys, letters of intent, waitlists, or pilot results?
findings.suggestion: You've nailed the market sizing and competitive landscape, but you need to show investors you've actually talked to buyers and agents in Dubai who experience this pain. Add a new slide after your Problem slide with 3-4 specific findings from customer interviews—for example, "Interviewed 12 property buyers in Dubai; 11 reported managing documents across 3+ platforms; average deal timeline extended 6 weeks due to manual KYC re-submission across agents" or include a quote from an agent about the friction they see daily. This transforms your 99% statistic from market research into lived customer reality.

findings.label: Does the deck clearly define the target customer segment with specific characteristics — not just broad demographics?
findings.suggestion: You're showing massive market opportunity but haven't told investors who you're actually going after first. Add a dedicated slide that names your primary customer segment with 3-4 specific characteristics—for example: "Mid-market real estate agents in Dubai (AED 50M–500M annual transaction volume) who handle 10+ deals monthly and currently use fragmented tools for due diligence." This tells investors you've thought through who pays first and why they're desperate for your solution, not just that the PropTech market is growing.

findings.label: Does the deck explain why this problem is urgent or why now is the right time to solve it?
findings.suggestion: You have the H1 2025 +25% YoY growth data but it's buried in your citations—pull it front and center on your Problem slide to show the market is accelerating right now. Add a line like "Dubai property transactions surged 25% in H1 2025, creating urgent demand for solutions that can handle this volume spike" so investors see this isn't a slow-burn problem you're solving eventually, it's a bottleneck that's getting worse every quarter and creating immediate pain for buyers and agents today.

findings.label: Does the deck address any regulatory or compliance factors relevant to the market?
Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: Powered by Claude AI (Anthropic) — search, legal review, risk flagging, daily follow-up. RAG-only verified responses. Anthropic Claude API (LLM & AI Engine) . RAG pipeline · Intent parser · Context retrieval City Specific Trained SLM → School catchments · Commute isochrones POI discovery · Hyperlocal pricing. Property suitability scoring, buyer intent prediction, mortgage probability scoring, developer inventory matching. Dubai-trained Small Language Model: school catchments, commute isochrones, POI discovery, hyperlocal pricing. Intent + context-aware. Sub-200ms SLA gate mandatory before any user sees results.

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: City-tuned SLM → Dubai-trained Small Language Model: school catchments, commute isochrones, POI discovery, hyperlocal pricing. Own hyperlocal map (planned)
findings.suggestion: Your Dubai-trained SLM is your strongest moat candidate, but you're burying it in a tech stack list—investors need to see *why* it's defensible and *how hard* it is to replicate. Add a slide that shows: (1) the specific proprietary data you've collected to train it (e.g., "6 months of DLD transaction patterns + 2,000 hyperlocal POI datasets Dubai agents don't have access to"), (2) how much training data and time it would take a competitor to match it (e.g., "18+ months to gather equivalent school catchment + commute isochrone coverage across Dubai's 50+ neighborhoods"), and

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: Production-ready stack — live pre-funding · RERA licence active · AML/KYC (PDPL) built-in from day one · Sub-200ms search SLA mandatory gate

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: AI-guided advisors for mortgage decisions, legal review, site visits, and DLD transfer appointments. Real people, live data.
findings.suggestion: Right now your tech slide shows what you built, but investors need to see the buyer's actual journey—walk them through a specific example like "A Dubai expat searches for a 2BR near DIFC, AI surfaces 3 matching properties with school catchments and commute times, buyer clicks to schedule a site visit, your consultant confirms the legal status via DLD API and flags title risks, then handles the mortgage pre-approval call." This concrete sequence shows how your AI and human layers work together in real time, not as separate components.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.evidence: Powered by Claude AI (Anthropic) — search, legal review, risk flagging, daily follow-up. RAG-only verified responses. Anthropic Claude API (LLM & AI Engine)

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.evidence: AML/KYC (PDPL) built-in from day one · Sub-200ms search SLA mandatory gate

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: LTV = ARPA ÷ churn rate · Blended CAC = S&M spend ÷ new paying customers · 10% annual subscription churn

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to this slide stating your current monthly burn rate and how many months of runway you have left — something like "Current burn: $180K/month | Runway: 14 months (as of Q1 2025)" positioned near the top. This tells investors exactly how much time you have to hit your milestones and whether this raise gets you to profitability or the next inflection point.

findings.label: Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: CAC: $2,100 (2026) → ~$800 (2028) · network effects drive 62% reduction. 16 : 1 LTV:CAC (Year 3). LTV by customer segment: Developer ~$272,500, B2C Buyer ~$35,000+, Homeowner ~$49,000, Broker/Agent ~$27,250

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: Revenue $2.8M (24 txn/yr → $22.3M (108 txn/yr) in 4 Years appears consistently stated twice in the provided slide content with identical figures
findings.suggestion: Good news—your numbers are locked in consistently across the slide, which is exactly what investors want to see. Now add one line showing how you'll actually hit those transaction volumes: for example, "24 txn/yr requires 8 enterprise developer partnerships + 500 homeowner signups; 108 txn/yr assumes 40 partnerships + 8K homeowners" so investors understand the path isn't just math, it's grounded in real customer acquisition milestones.

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.suggestion: Add a new slide right after this one that shows exactly how you'll deploy the capital you're raising — break it down into buckets like "Product & Engineering ($X)", "Sales & Marketing ($X)", "Operations ($X)" and tie each to a specific milestone (like "hire 3 engineers to build API integrations by Q2" or "allocate $500K to developer acquisition campaigns targeting the $272K LTV segment"). This gives investors confidence you've thought through execution, not just the financial destination.

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
findings.suggestion: You're showing strong unit economics and a 43% EBITDA target by 2030, but investors need to see the exact quarter or year you hit breakeven—add a callout like "Breakeven: Q3 2027" or "Cash flow positive by Year 2.5" directly on this slide so it's impossible to miss. Right now they have to reverse-engineer it from your revenue and EBITDA curves, which means some will just skip that work.

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.suggestion: Right now you're showing milestones and transaction counts, but investors need to see actual paying customers and revenue already happening, not targets. Replace "72 transactions" and "$2.8M revenue target" with what's real today — for example, "48 completed transactions generating $340K in platform fees from 12 paying brokerages" or whatever your current live revenue actually is, then show the targets separately as your growth plan.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: One Platform. Four Markets. Sequenced for Capital Efficiency. Single configurable localisation engine — no platform rebuild per market. UAE proves the model. Every subsequent market is a deployment.

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.suggestion: You've got milestones and revenue targets scattered across the roadmap, but investors need to see the growth trajectory right now—pull out your actual UAE traction (you mention 72 transactions and a $2.8M revenue target, but what are you at today?) and show month-over-month growth for the last 3-6 months as a separate metric on this slide, like "March: 12 txns → April: 18 txns → May: 28 txns" to prove the model is accelerating before you scale to other markets. This proves the unit economics work before you ask for capital to replicate it.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.suggestion: Right now your roadmap shows acquisition milestones (agents, transactions, revenue targets) but investors need to see that users are staying and finding value—add one metric to each phase showing either agent retention rate or transaction repeat rate, like "72 transactions from 200 agents (36% repeat rate)" in UAE phase one, then track how that improves as you scale. This proves your model works beyond just signing people up.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
findings.suggestion: You've got real traction numbers here—72 transactions, 200 agents, $2.8M revenue target—but investors won't know if these are from actual customers or projections. Add one concrete win to this slide: name a specific brokerage or developer that's live on your platform in UAE, or pull out one agent's story (e.g., "Agent Fatima closed 12 properties in Q1 2026 using E·GO's transaction engine"). This transforms the roadmap from ambitious to credible.

findings.label: Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?
findings.evidence: SKIPPED: Target sections confirmed missing by Pass 1

findings.label: Does the competitive analysis appear superficial — for example a magic quadrant where the company leads in all dimensions without explanation?
findings.evidence: SKIPPED: Target sections confirmed missing by Pass 1

findings.label: Does the deck address the threat of large incumbents or well-funded competitors entering the space?
findings.evidence: SKIPPED: Target sections confirmed missing by Pass 1

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: Ariz Rizvi Founder & CEO · Zeevaa... Jaimohan Pillai CTO · Zeevaa... Sales Lead (UAE) Month 1... Mortgage / FinTech Mgr Month 2–3
findings.suggestion: You're showing two open roles (Sales Lead and Mortgage Manager) launching months 1-3, which signals you're still building out leadership—that's fine, but investors need to see who's actively recruiting and what experience they bring to hiring for these gaps. Add a line under your name or in a team section stating something like "Ariz leading sales hiring; target: 15+ years B2B fintech sales experience" so it's clear you're not waiting passively for these roles to fill.

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: Ariz Rizvi: 20+ industry experience, London Business School MBA, $150M+ in completed UAE real estate transactions, Active RERA Broker Licence. Jaimohan Pillai: Ex-Lloyds, and Barclays, from UK has over 3 decades of experience in Data science, Senior industry executive with 30+ years across real estate, financial services, and enterprise technology in the UAE and wider GCC.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.evidence: Advisory board to be formed post-seed
findings.suggestion: You need to name at least 2-3 people who've already committed to advising you—even informally—before you close this round. Investors want to see that experienced people believe in your vision enough to help you now, not after you have their money. If you have a former VP of Sales from a competitor, an engineer who built something similar, or an operator from a relevant company who's willing to be listed, add them with a one-line credential that shows why they matter for your specific business.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to your team slide stating something like "Founders: 4-year vest with 1-year cliff" — investors always ask this in the room, and having it visible shows you've thought through retention and alignment from day one.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: RERA Licensed Only AI platform legally authorised to collect commissions & hold escrow — a regulatory moat no tech competitor holds. RERA Broker Licence: Ariz Rizvi Real Estate LLC
findings.suggestion: You've got a genuine competitive advantage here that investors in regulated spaces care deeply about — your RERA license and escrow authority are real moats — but they're buried or missing entirely from your deck. Add a dedicated slide or section showing your regulatory status clearly: "RERA Licensed Broker (Ariz Rizvi Real Estate LLC) — legally authorized to hold escrow and collect commissions," then explain why this matters (competitors can't do this without years of licensing, it reduces fraud risk for users, it unlocks revenue streams others can't access). This transforms a compliance checkbox into a defensibility story.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.suggestion: You don't need to add a slide about this unless you actually have pending litigation or IP disputes—but if you do, you need to disclose it now rather than let investors discover it in diligence. If there's anything gray (a cease-and-desist letter, a trademark challenge, a patent claim someone's hinted at), add one bullet to your legal/risk slide saying exactly what it is, when it started, and your legal counsel's assessment of the likelihood and potential impact.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.suggestion: No issues found here — your numbers are internally consistent across the deck. Keep spot-checking as you update slides, since even small changes to one metric (like adjusting your TAM or revising last quarter's revenue) can accidentally break alignment elsewhere.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: Only AI platform legally authorised to collect commissions & hold escrow — a regulatory moat no tech competitor holds.

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.suggestion: I don't have any slide content to review for formatting issues, so I can't give you a specific fix. Pull up your actual deck and do a quick pass: check that all fonts, colors, and spacing match across slides, run spell-check, and make sure there's no "INSERT X HERE" or Lorem Ipsum text hiding anywhere. Even one typo or mismatched font size signals to investors that you weren't detail-oriented enough to catch it before the meeting.
